walkthrough.title: Unfold the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Maracaibo set up. First, unfold the large main board and place it in the center of the table. This is the map of the Caribbean showing a circular rondel track of coastal villages around the sea. You will also see three nation influence columns on the side for Spain, England, and France.

walkthrough.title: Place Village Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now let's populate the villages. Place the appropriate tokens on each village space as indicated in the rulebook setup diagram. Different villages offer different action types, so the tokens help you identify what each village does at a glance.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Exploration Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the exploration tokens and place them face down on the inland exploration areas of the map. These represent undiscovered jungle and mountain regions that players can explore during the game. Do not peek at them.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Influence Tracks
walkthrough.text: Look at the three nation columns on the board for Spain, England, and France. Each track has numbered spaces going upward. Place a neutral marker at the starting position of each track. Players will advance their own markers on these tracks during the game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Project Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the large deck of project cards. These are multi-use cards you will play throughout the game for actions, combat strength, or money. Give them a thorough shuffle and place the deck face down near the board.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Card Display
walkthrough.text: Flip the top cards from the project deck face up to create the card display row. The exact number of face-up cards depends on your player count. Players will draft from this display during the game, and it refills from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Quest Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the quest cards into their own face-down deck and place it near the board. Quest cards provide side objectives with VP rewards. During the game, players can acquire quests from certain village actions.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Doubloon Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the doubloon coins in a shared supply near the board where everyone can reach them. Doubloons are the currency of the game, used for upgrades, crew hiring, card acquisition, and certain village actions.

walkthrough.title: Organize Building Tokens and Combat Tokens
walkthrough.text: Sort the building tokens by type and place them in a shared supply. Do the same with the combat tokens. Buildings get placed in villages for area influence, and combat tokens are used during privateering encounters.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on space one of the round track. Maracaibo is played over exactly four rounds. Each round represents one full voyage around the Caribbean rondel.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player their own player board. This board tracks your ship upgrades, crew slots, and personal achievements. Take a moment to look it over. You will see spaces for cannon, cargo, navigation, and crew quarter upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Place Ships on the Starting Village
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a ship token in their chosen color and places it on the starting village of the rondel track. All ships begin at the same location. This is where your voyage begins each round.

walkthrough.title: Take Influence Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes three influence markers in their color, one for each nation track. Place them at the starting position on the Spain, England, and France columns. You will advance these during the game to score VP.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Cards and Doubloons
walkthrough.text: Deal each player a starting hand of project cards. Then distribute starting doubloons based on turn order. Players going later in the turn order receive slightly more doubloons to compensate for the disadvantage of acting second.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Setup (Optional)
walkthrough.text: If your group is playing the campaign mode, now is the time to set it up. Prepare the story cards and any legacy elements from previous sessions. The campaign is non-destructive, meaning no components are permanently altered and the game fully resets for standard play. If you are not playing the campaign, skip this step entirely.

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a random first player. Play will proceed clockwise. The first player will move their ship first on the rondel this round. You are all set up and ready to sail the Caribbean. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Maracaibo is played over four rounds. Each round represents a voyage around the Caribbean. Players take turns moving their ships forward along the rondel track of villages, performing actions at each stop. When all ships have reached the end of the rondel, the round ends, scoring occurs, and everyone starts a new voyage.

walkthrough.title: The Rondel Track
walkthrough.text: The rondel is the ring of coastal villages around the Caribbean map. On your turn, you must move your ship forward one to seven spaces. You can never move backward. You only perform the action of the village where you stop, so any villages you skip are lost for this round. Think of it like a one-way road with rest stops.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Your turn has three parts. First, move your ship forward one to seven spaces on the rondel. Second, perform the action of the village where you land. Third, optionally play project cards from your hand for their effects. That is it. Simple on the surface, but the decisions run deep.

walkthrough.title: Village Action Types
walkthrough.text: Different villages offer different actions. Market villages let you buy or sell goods and acquire new project cards. Port villages let you hire crew and upgrade your ship. Settlement villages let you place buildings for area influence. Exploration villages let you launch inland expeditions. And combat villages let you engage in privateering.

walkthrough.title: Movement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Here is where the tension lies. Moving more spaces reaches distant valuable villages but skips everything in between. Moving fewer spaces gives you more stops but slows your progress. And watch the other players. If someone races ahead, they might end the round before you reach all your planned stops. Balancing speed and thoroughness is the core puzzle of the rondel.

walkthrough.title: Project Cards
walkthrough.text: These are the most versatile tool in the game. Every project card can be used in three different ways. You can play it for its printed action effect. You can use it for combat strength during privateering encounters. Or you can discard it for doubloons. Choosing how to use each card is one of the biggest decisions you will make.

walkthrough.title: The Three Nations
walkthrough.text: The board has three influence tracks for Spain, England, and France. Advancing on any track grants escalating benefits and VP. You gain influence through specific village actions, card plays, and winning combats. At the end of each round, players score VP based on their positions on all three tracks.

walkthrough.title: Influence Scoring
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round, you score VP based on where your markers sit on the nation tracks. Higher positions earn more points. Additionally, having the highest influence in a nation earns bonus VP. Since this happens four times, even a small lead compounds dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Ship Upgrades
walkthrough.text: Your player board has upgrade slots for your ship. There are four types: cannons improve combat strength, cargo increases trading capacity, navigation enhances movement options, and crew quarters let you hire more crew members. Upgrades are permanent once purchased. They stay for the rest of the game, making your ship progressively more powerful each round.

walkthrough.title: Hiring Crew
walkthrough.text: At port villages, you can hire crew members. Each crew tile provides a unique ongoing ability. Your ship has limited crew capacity, but you can expand it through crew quarter upgrades. Choose crew that synergize with your strategy. Combat crew for privateers, trade crew for merchants, exploration crew for explorers.

walkthrough.title: Exploration
walkthrough.text: From certain villages, you can explore the inland jungle and mountain areas of the Caribbean. When you explore, you flip face-down tokens that provide resources, VP, or special bonuses. There is also an exploration track that grants cumulative benefits as you advance. Exploration rewards are unique and can not be obtained any other way.

walkthrough.title: Combat and Privateering
walkthrough.text: At combat villages, you can attack colonial targets. Combat strength comes from your project cards and cannon upgrades. You commit cards to the fight, and higher strength earns better rewards including influence advancement and VP. The risk is spending cards that could have been used elsewhere, so weigh the reward carefully.

walkthrough.title: Quests
walkthrough.text: Quest cards are side objectives that give you specific goals, such as reaching a certain influence level or exploring specific areas. Completing quests grants significant VP. You can acquire quests throughout the game. Some quests are easier to complete early, while others require late-game development. Look at your quests early and plan your route around them.

walkthrough.title: Building Placement
walkthrough.text: At settlement villages, you can place buildings. Buildings give you area influence in that region of the map. Having the most buildings in a region scores end-game VP through majority scoring. Buildings can also provide adjacency bonuses when placed near other buildings. This is a long-term investment that pays off at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Doubloons
walkthrough.text: These are the game's currency. You spend them on upgrades, crew, card acquisition, and certain village actions. You earn doubloons through trade, card effects, and village actions. One important detail: leftover doubloons do not convert to VP at the end of the game, so spend them wisely before the final round ends.

walkthrough.title: Round End
walkthrough.text: A round ends when all players' ships have reached or passed the final village on the rondel. When this happens, scoring occurs for influence positions on all three nation tracks. Then all ships return to the starting village, the round marker advances, and a new voyage begins.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Pacing
walkthrough.text: One of the unique dynamics of Maracaibo is tempo pressure. If one player rushes ahead on the rondel, the round can end before slower players reach their planned stops. You must constantly weigh whether to take more village actions or race forward to avoid being cut off. Players who control the pace have a significant strategic advantage.

walkthrough.title: Escalating Power
walkthrough.text: As rounds progress, your ship becomes more powerful. Upgrades, crew, and influence all compound. A ship that felt limited in round one becomes a well-oiled machine by round three. This means your early decisions about which upgrades and crew to invest in shape your entire game. Build your foundation carefully.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Mode Overview
walkthrough.text: Maracaibo includes an optional campaign mode with legacy-style story elements. Story cards introduce narrative events that affect game conditions. Your choices carry forward between sessions, creating an evolving experience. The campaign is non-destructive, so no components are permanently altered. You can fully reset the game for standard play anytime.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the fourth round of scoring. In addition to the VP earned from influence each round, you now tally final scoring categories: quest VP from completed quest cards, building VP from placed buildings, exploration VP from your exploration track and discovered tokens, crew and ship upgrade VP, and project card VP from cards in your tableau. The player with the most total VP wins. If there is a tie, the player with more remaining doubloons wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Caribbean
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Player 1 is focused on influence, Player 2 is an explorer, and Player 3 will make some common beginner mistakes so you can learn from them. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: All three ships start at the same village at the beginning of the rondel. Each player has their starting hand of project cards and their starting doubloons. Nobody has any crew, upgrades, or influence yet. The rondel stretches out ahead with villages offering different action types. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: A Short Move to a Market
walkthrough.text: Player 1 moves their ship forward just two spaces to land on a market village. At the market, Player 1 acquires a new project card from the display. This card has a strong influence-boosting effect that will be useful later. Player 1 also has the option to buy goods. A short move means more stops this round.

walkthrough.title: Why Short Moves Matter Early
walkthrough.text: Player 1 only moved two spaces, which means they will have many more stops available this round. In the early game, visiting more villages lets you build your foundation. You want to grab cards, start influence tracks, and pick up quests before racing ahead. Short moves early, longer moves later is a reliable pattern.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Moving to an Exploration Village
walkthrough.text: Here, Player 2 launches an inland expedition, flipping an exploration token. The token reveals a resource bonus. Player 2 also advances one step on the exploration track, unlocking a small ongoing benefit. The exploration strategy is underway.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Jumping Too Far
walkthrough.text: Player 3 sees a lucrative combat village six spaces ahead and jumps straight to it. The problem is that Player 3 skipped a port village, a market village, and an exploration village along the way. Worse, the combat encounter requires strong cards that Player 3 does not have yet. Player 3 commits cards to the fight and earns a mediocre result.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Rush the Rondel
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is critical. Jumping too far on the rondel in round one wastes opportunities. You skip villages you can never come back to, and you may arrive at a village you are not prepared for. In the first round, it is almost always better to take short moves and build your capabilities before attempting big plays.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Advancing Influence
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays the project card acquired earlier to boost their Spain influence by two steps. This puts Player 1 in a strong position for end-of-round scoring on that track. Remember, influence scoring happens every round, so early leads compound.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Hiring Crew
walkthrough.text: This crew tile will give Player 2 a bonus every time they explore for the rest of the game. Crew members are permanent, so hiring early maximizes their value across all four rounds.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Influence
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has been focused entirely on combat and has neglected the influence tracks. All three markers are still at the starting position. When end-of-round scoring happens, Player 3 will earn zero VP from influence while the other players earn points. Since influence scores every round, this is like falling behind on compound interest.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Influence Is Not Optional
walkthrough.text: Ignoring the influence tracks is one of the costliest beginner mistakes. Influence scores every single round, so a player who advances influence early earns that VP four times over. Even if your main strategy is exploration or combat, you should still advance at least one or two nation tracks. The recurring VP adds up enormously.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Playing Cards Wisely
walkthrough.text: Player 1 lands on another village and plays a project card for its action effect rather than discarding it for doubloons. The card provides both influence and a small VP bonus. Player 1 carefully considered all three uses of the card and chose the one that aligned with their strategy. This is the heart of hand management in Maracaibo.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Discarding Everything for Doubloons
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is running low on doubloons after the combat attempt and starts discarding project cards for money. The problem is that those cards had powerful action effects that are now permanently lost. Discarding for doubloons should be a last resort, not a primary strategy. Player 3 is trading long-term value for short-term cash.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Cards Are Worth More Than Cash
walkthrough.text: A project card's action effect is almost always more valuable than the doubloons you get from discarding it. Think of discarding as an emergency option. Before you discard a card, ask yourself: could this card give me influence, VP, or a strategic advantage if I played it for its effect instead? Most of the time, the answer is yes.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Building in a Settlement
walkthrough.text: This building gives Player 2 area influence in this region. If Player 2 ends the game with the most buildings here, they earn majority VP. Building placement is a long-term investment that pays off at the very end.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Picking Up a Quest
walkthrough.text: Player 1 stops at a village where quests are available. After reviewing the options, Player 1 takes a quest that aligns with their influence strategy: reach a certain level on two nation tracks. This quest is very achievable given Player 1's current trajectory and will score significant bonus VP.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Taking Quests You Cannot Complete
walkthrough.text: Player 3 also grabs a quest, but picks one requiring high exploration track progress. The problem is that Player 3 has done zero exploration and has no crew or upgrades that support it. This quest will sit unfulfilled, earning zero VP. When picking quests, choose ones that match what you are already doing, not ones that require a completely new strategy.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Align Quests With Your Strategy
walkthrough.text: Quests are powerful VP sources, but only if you complete them. Take quests that reinforce your existing direction. If you are pushing influence, grab an influence quest. If you are exploring, grab an exploration quest. A completed three-point quest is infinitely better than an uncompleted eight-point quest.

walkthrough.title: Approaching the End of the Rondel
walkthrough.text: As the round progresses, all three ships approach the final stretch of the rondel. Player 1 has made steady short moves and visited many villages, building a strong foundation of influence and cards. Player 2 has explored, hired crew, and placed a building. Player 3 has fewer accomplishments due to the rushed early move and wasted cards.

walkthrough.title: End of Round One: Influence Scoring
walkthrough.text: All ships reach the final village and the round ends. Now we score influence. Player 1 scores the most VP thanks to their lead on the Spain track and decent positions on England and France. Player 2 scores moderate VP from balanced influence. Player 3 scores almost nothing because they neglected the tracks entirely. This gap will widen every round.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Redemption Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 3 reviews their position. The lessons are clear: take shorter moves early, invest in influence, use cards for effects instead of cash, and choose quests that match your strategy. In round two, Player 3 plans to advance at least two nation tracks before attempting any combat, and to be more selective about movement and card use.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Ships return to the starting village for a new voyage. Your crew, upgrades, and influence carry forward. This time around, you know where the valuable villages are and you have a better sense of pacing. Experienced players often vary their route each round, targeting different village types based on their evolving needs.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen movement, influence, exploration, combat, crew hiring, building placement, quest selection, and card management. Remember the key lessons: do not rush the rondel, invest in influence early, use your cards for their effects, and pick quests that match your strategy. You are ready to sail on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Set Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to village types, card uses, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Set Sail
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for village types and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common beginner mistakes. First, rushing the rondel by jumping five or six spaces in round one instead of taking short moves to build your foundation. Second, ignoring influence tracks, which score every round and are the single biggest source of VP. Third, discarding project cards for doubloons when their action effects are far more valuable. Fourth, taking quests that do not match your current strategy. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Foundation Building
walkthrough.text: Your first round sets the tone for the entire game. Focus on three things: advancing at least one influence track, hiring your first crew member, and acquiring useful project cards. Resist the urge to attempt combat or exploration before you have the cards and upgrades to support them. Think of round one as laying the keel of your ship. Everything you build later depends on this foundation.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Influence Early
walkthrough.text: Influence scoring happens at the end of every round, which means early investment pays dividends four times. A player who advances two steps on a nation track in round one earns those VP in rounds one, two, three, and four. A player who waits until round three to start influence only earns VP twice. Pick one or two nations to focus on and start pushing them immediately.

walkthrough.title: Crew Is Your Best Investment
walkthrough.text: Crew members provide ongoing abilities that fire every time you take certain actions. Hiring crew in round one means you benefit from their ability for all four rounds. Hiring the same crew in round three means you only benefit for two rounds. Prioritize crew that aligns with your strategy. An exploration crew member for an explorer, a combat crew for a privateer, and a trading crew for a merchant.

walkthrough.title: Read the Board Before You Move
walkthrough.text: Before advancing your ship, look ahead at the next several villages. Identify which ones you want to visit and plan your movement to hit as many as possible. This is especially important in later rounds when you need to be more selective. A good rondel plan considers not just what you need, but what your opponents want. Landing on a village before them can deny key actions.

walkthrough.title: Use Project Cards for Effects
walkthrough.text: Every project card has three uses, and new players tend to default to discarding for doubloons. Fight that instinct. The action effect of a card almost always generates more long-term value than the doubloons. Save discarding for when you genuinely have no better option. Think of your hand as a toolbox: each card is a specific tool for a specific job.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Tempo
walkthrough.text: One of the unique skills in Maracaibo is reading the tempo of the round. If you see an opponent making big jumps, they might end the round before you reach your planned stops. In that case, you may need to abandon a low-priority village to make sure you hit your high-priority ones. Conversely, if everyone is moving slowly, you have the luxury of taking every stop. Stay aware of where other ships are.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Upgrades
walkthrough.text: Ship upgrades are permanent and compound over the game. Navigation upgrades help you reach more villages per round. Cannon upgrades make combat more rewarding. Cargo upgrades improve trade income. Crew quarter upgrades let you hire more crew. Choose upgrades that reinforce your strategy rather than spreading thin. Two or three focused upgrades are better than one of everything.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't rush the rondel in round 1 — take short moves, build your foundation
Don't ignore influence — it scores every round and compounds
Don't discard cards for doubloons — action effects are more valuable
Don't take quests misaligned with your strategy

summary.title: Round One: Foundation Building
bullets: Advance at least 1 influence track
Hire your first crew member
Acquire useful project cards
Avoid combat/exploration before you have support

summary.title: Invest in Influence Early
bullets: Influence scores every round — early leads pay 4x
Pick 1-2 nations to focus on, push immediately
Waiting until round 3 = only 2 rounds of VP

summary.title: Crew Is Your Best Investment
bullets: Crew abilities fire every relevant action for the rest of the game
Hiring in round 1 = 4 rounds of benefit vs. round 3 = 2 rounds
Match crew to strategy: exploration, combat, or trade

summary.title: Read the Board Before You Move
bullets: Plan movement to hit as many desired villages as possible
Consider what opponents want — landing first can deny actions
Especially important in later rounds when selectivity matters

summary.title: Use Project Cards for Effects
bullets: Action effects > doubloons in almost all cases
Discard only as a last resort
Treat your hand as a toolbox with specific tools

summary.title: Watch the Tempo
bullets: If opponents make big jumps, they may end the round early
Abandon low-priority stops to secure high-priority ones
When everyone moves slowly, take every stop you can

summary.title: Plan Your Upgrades
bullets: Upgrades are permanent and compound over the game
Focus on 2-3 upgrade types that reinforce your strategy
Spreading thin across all types is less effective

walkthrough.title: Influence Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: The influence dominance approach focuses on maximizing your position on one or two nation tracks for massive recurring VP. Since influence scores every round, a player with a strong lead earns that advantage four times over. Start by targeting villages that grant influence advancement and play project cards that boost your chosen nations. Pair this with crew members that provide influence bonuses. By round three, your lead should be large enough that other players cannot catch up.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Influence Returns
walkthrough.text: The key to influence dominance is focusing on one nation heavily and one moderately rather than spreading across all three. Majority bonuses for the top position in a nation are significant, so aim for first place in your primary nation. Use combat encounters strategically, as winning combats often grants influence as a reward. Time your biggest influence pushes for early rounds so the recurring VP compounds as much as possible.

walkthrough.title: Explorer Strategy
walkthrough.text: The exploration-heavy approach targets inland expedition villages to uncover discovery tokens and advance the exploration track. Exploration rewards are unique and cannot be replicated through other actions. Prioritize navigation upgrades for better movement and exploration crew for bonuses when exploring. The exploration track grants cumulative benefits that make each subsequent expedition more rewarding.

walkthrough.title: Deep Exploration Tactics
walkthrough.text: To maximize exploration returns, plan your rondel route around exploration villages each round. Hire exploration crew as early as possible so their bonuses apply to every expedition. Navigation upgrades help you reach exploration villages without skipping other valuable stops. The discovery tokens provide a mix of immediate resources and end-game VP. In later rounds, deeper exploration areas often have the most valuable tokens.

walkthrough.title: Privateer Strategy
walkthrough.text: The privateer approach uses combat encounters for both direct VP and rapid influence advancement. Combat strength comes from project cards and cannon upgrades. The key advantage of privateering is that it advances influence while also earning VP and resources, essentially doing two things at once. Cannon upgrades make every combat more efficient.

walkthrough.title: Efficient Combat
walkthrough.text: The biggest trap for privateers is over-committing cards to combat. You want to win by the minimum necessary margin, not waste your best cards on an already-won fight. Cannon upgrades reduce the number of cards you need to commit. Build your hand management around having combat-ready cards available when you reach combat villages while preserving action-effect cards for other uses.

walkthrough.title: Quest Specialist Strategy
walkthrough.text: The quest specialist aims to complete as many quests as possible for their significant VP rewards. The key is acquiring quests early and planning your rondel route around fulfilling them. Take quests that overlap with each other and with your other scoring paths. For example, if you are pushing Spain influence, take quests that reward Spain influence milestones. Completing three or four quests can easily swing the game.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Strategies
walkthrough.text: The strongest players in Maracaibo rarely commit to a single pure strategy. Instead, they build a primary engine and supplement it with a secondary scoring path. An influence player who also completes two quests, or an explorer who also builds in key settlements, will outscore a one-dimensional player. The key is to identify which secondary path requires the least additional investment given your primary direction.

walkthrough.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
walkthrough.text: Your strategy should evolve across the four rounds. Round one is for foundation: hire crew, start influence, acquire cards. Round two is for expansion: begin your primary strategy in earnest, take quests. Round three is the pivot: shift from building toward scoring, complete quests. Round four is the sprint: maximize VP with every move, spend remaining doubloons on upgrades and actions. The best players plan their round four route during round three.

walkthrough.title: Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use tempo as a weapon. If you are ahead on influence, racing through the rondel forces scoring before opponents can catch up. If you are behind, moving slowly gives you more village stops to close the gap. Watch your opponents' positions and adjust your pace. Sometimes the best move is a big jump to force an early round end, and sometimes it is a one-space crawl to squeeze out every last action. Reading the tempo separates good players from great ones.

entries.definition: The circular track of coastal villages around the Caribbean map. Players move their ships forward along this track each turn, stopping at villages to perform actions. Ships can only move forward, never backward.

A location on the rondel track where players can stop and perform actions. Each village has a specific type (market, port, settlement, exploration, or combat) determining what action is available there.
One full voyage around the Caribbean rondel. The game consists of four rounds. Each round ends when all ships reach or pass the final village, followed by influence scoring.
On your turn, you must move your ship forward one to seven spaces on the rondel. You cannot move backward. You only act at your destination village; skipped villages are unavailable this round.
The pace at which players move around the rondel. A player who moves quickly can force the round to end before slower players reach their planned stops. Tempo control is an advanced strategic tool.

Multi-use cards that form the backbone of player interaction. Each card can be used three ways: played for its action effect, committed for combat strength in privateering, or discarded for doubloons.
A row of face-up project cards available for acquisition. Players can draft cards from the display at market villages. The display refills from the deck as cards are taken.
Side objective cards with specific goals such as reaching influence milestones or exploring specific areas. Completed quests grant significant VP rewards. Acquired from certain village actions.
Campaign-mode cards that introduce narrative events affecting game conditions. Story cards are only used during the legacy campaign and do not appear in standard play.

The primary ability printed on a project card. Playing a card for its action effect provides benefits like influence advancement, resources, exploration bonuses, or VP. Generally the most valuable use of a card.
One of three ways to use a project card. Discarding a card generates doubloons but loses the card's action effect permanently. Should be used as a last resort when other card uses are not viable.
One of the three nation influence tracks. Advancing on the Spain track grants escalating benefits and contributes to end-of-round VP scoring. Players compete for majority bonuses.
One of the three nation influence tracks. Advancing on the England track grants escalating benefits and contributes to end-of-round VP scoring. Players compete for majority bonuses.
One of the three nation influence tracks. Advancing on the France track grants escalating benefits and contributes to end-of-round VP scoring. Players compete for majority bonuses.
A column on the board representing a player's standing with a colonial power. Higher positions earn more VP during end-of-round scoring. Three tracks exist: Spain, England, and France.
VP awarded at the end of each round based on players' positions on the three nation tracks. Higher positions score more VP, and majority holders earn bonus VP. Occurs four times per game.
Extra VP awarded to the player with the highest influence on a nation track during end-of-round scoring. Ties may split the bonus. Majority bonuses are a key motivator for influence competition.
A village type where players can buy or sell goods and acquire project cards from the display. Markets are essential for building and refreshing your hand of project cards.
A village type where players can hire crew members and purchase ship upgrades. Ports are the primary source of permanent improvements to your ship's capabilities.
A village type where players can place buildings for area influence. Buildings contribute to majority scoring in regions at the end of the game.
A village type that allows players to launch inland expeditions. Exploring reveals face-down tokens with resources, VP, or special bonuses, and advances the exploration track.
A village type where players can engage in privateering encounters against colonial targets. Combat uses card strength and cannon upgrades, rewarding influence and VP.

The currency of the game. Used for ship upgrades, crew hiring, card acquisition, and certain village actions. Earned through trade, card effects, and village actions. Remaining doubloons do not convert to VP at game end.
Tokens placed in settlement villages to establish area influence. Having the most buildings in a region scores majority VP at the end of the game. Buildings may also provide adjacency bonuses.
Face-down tokens on the inland areas of the map. When a player explores, they flip a token to reveal resources, VP, or special bonuses. Once revealed, they cannot be explored again.
Tokens used during privateering encounters at combat villages. They represent the colonial targets being attacked and determine the rewards for winning combat.
A player's marker on the rondel track. Each player has one ship that moves forward along the rondel each turn. All ships start at the same village at the beginning of each round.
Each player's personal board tracking ship upgrades, crew members, and achievements. The board has slots for cannon, cargo, navigation, and crew quarter upgrades.

Hired crew members that provide unique ongoing abilities for the rest of the game. Each crew tile occupies a crew slot on your player board. Crew capacity is limited but expandable through crew quarter upgrades.
A player-colored marker placed on each of the three nation tracks. Moved upward as the player gains influence through village actions, card plays, and combat.
A ship upgrade that improves combat strength. Cannon upgrades reduce the number of project cards you need to commit to win combat encounters, making privateering more efficient.
A ship upgrade that increases trading capacity. Cargo upgrades improve the economic returns from trade-related village actions and card effects.
A ship upgrade that enhances movement options on the rondel. Navigation upgrades help you reach more villages per round without skipping valuable stops.
A ship upgrade that expands your crew capacity. Each crew quarters upgrade allows you to hire additional crew members beyond your base limit.
A track on the board that advances as players explore inland areas. Higher positions on the exploration track grant cumulative benefits, making each subsequent expedition more rewarding.
A track showing the current round number (one through four). The round marker advances after each round of play. The game ends after the fourth round.
A marker used to track victory points on the scoring track. VP is earned from influence scoring, combat, quests, and various other sources throughout the game.
Victory points earned at the end of each round based on positions on the three nation tracks. The primary recurring source of VP in the game. Compounds over four rounds.
Victory points earned by completing quest cards. Quest VP is scored at the end of the game. Quests provide some of the largest single VP awards available.
Victory points earned at the end of the game from buildings placed in villages. Majority scoring awards VP to the player with the most buildings in each region.
Victory points earned from the exploration track position and from discovered exploration tokens. Scored at the end of the game.
Victory points from project cards in your tableau that provide end-game scoring. Some cards award VP based on conditions met during the game.
Some crew members provide end-game victory points in addition to their ongoing abilities. The VP value is printed on the crew tile.

An optional legacy-style game mode featuring a narrative story that evolves over multiple play sessions. Story cards introduce events and choices that carry forward. The campaign is non-destructive — no components are permanently altered, and the game fully resets for standard play.
The default game mode without campaign elements. All rules apply as normal without story cards or persistent narrative. Suitable for one-off plays or groups not interested in the campaign.
Maracaibo supports solo play at all player counts, including the campaign. An automated opponent system handles the non-player ships' movement and actions.
Extra benefits gained when placing buildings near other buildings in settlement villages. Adjacency can boost your area influence or provide additional resources.
Control established by placing buildings in village regions. The player with the most buildings in a region earns majority VP at the end of the game.
The skill of deciding how to use each project card in your hand. Since cards have three uses (action, combat, doubloons), choosing the optimal use in each situation is a core strategic skill.
Your personal collection of played project cards that remain in front of you. Some cards in your tableau provide ongoing effects or end-game VP scoring.
If two or more players have the same VP total at game end, the player with the most remaining doubloons wins the tiebreaker.
